source_metadata.title_raw: Peter Sullivan - I-19-233 - February 21, 2020 - San Francisco

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Peter Sullivan 
Compliance Manager 
S.E. Owens & Company 
Accounting and Compliance for 
February 21, 2020 
Political Non-Profit, and Animal Rights Organizations 
2352 Market St, 
San Francisco, CA 94114 
Re: 
Your Request for Informal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-19-233 
Dear Peter Sullivan: 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding campaign provisions of the 
Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Because your questions are general in nature, we are treating 
your inquiry as a request for informal assistance. 2 
Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTIONS 
1. If a slate mailer organization ("SMO") qualifies as a general purpose recipient committee by 
receiving contributions of $2,000 or more for the general production of slate mailers, does the 
SMO need to file a Statement oflntention (Form 410) to establish a general purpose committee 
with its own FPPC ID number? 
2. If the SMO is required to establish a recipient committee, is·all of the SM O's activity reported 
on the separate committee's campaign statement, for example the Form 460, or are just the 
income and expenses related to the general production contribution received required to be 
reported on the Form 460, with the SMO activity reported on the SMO's campaign statement 
(Form 401)? When will the recipient committee's obligation to file campaign reports end? 
1 Tht:1 Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329( c )(3).) 


CONCLUSIONS 
File No. 1-19-233 
Page No. 2 
1. Yes. The SMO would need to form a general purpose recipient committee by filing a Form 410 
and obtaining an FPPC ID number from the Secretary of State. 
2. The SMO would be required to file SMO Campaign Statements (Form 401) in addition to 
campaign statements for the new general purpose committee until the new committee no longer 
engages in reportable activity and is terminated. The committee's campaign statements would 
include the income and expenses related to the general production contribution received and 
must be attached to the SMO's campaign statement (Form 401). The SMO's Form 401 would 
continue to include SMO reportable activity. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
You are the Compliance Manager for S. E. Owens & Company and work on accounting for 
political non-profit and animal rights organizations. You have requested advice on behalf of an 
organization that has a slate mailer organization ("SMO") and a separate general purpose 
committee. The SMO has outstanding bills from the 2019 election and would like the general 
purpose committee to pay one of those bills (the bill is for more than $2,000). The existing general 
purpose committee and the SMO are managed by the same individuals. The existing general 
purpose committee has its own, separate purposes (i.e. making candidate contributions, producing 
their own mailers, IE campaigns). The SMO's debt was incurred for general costs to produce and 
distribute slate mailers, including printing, collecting voter data, postage and design. One slate 
mailer was for a citywide elec;tion and the other was targeting a specific district in the same 
election. No money received by the existing general purpose committee was received with any kind 
of agreement, direction, or indication that the money was intended for the purpose of going to the 
SMO or for production of slate mailers. 
ANALYSIS 
An SMO may qualify as a recipient committee if it receives "contributions" of $2,000 or 
more in a calendar year. (Section 82013.) A "contribution" means a payment, a forgiveness of a 
loan, a payment of a loan by a third party, or an enforceable promise to make a payment, except to 
the extent that full and adequate consideration is received or if it is clear from the surrounding 
circumstances that the payment is not made for political purposes. (~ection 82015.) 
Generally, payments received by a slate mailer organization for the purpose of including a 
particular candidate or measure on slate mailers are not "contributions" to the SMO because it is 
presumed that appearance in the slate mailer constitutes "full and adequate consideration." (Finley 
Advice Letter, No. A-92-219.) When a third party makes a payment to an SMO for a candidate or 
ballot measure to appear on slate mailers, that payment may be a contribution to the candidate or 
ballot measure committee. However, when an SMO receives an unearmarked payment to help 
defray the general costs of publishing slate mailers, no such consideration is provided in exchange 
for the payment, and such payments meet the Act's definition of a "contribution" to the SMO. 
(Finley Advice Letter, No. A-92-219.) 


FileNo. I-19-233 
Page No. 3 
Based on the facts provided, the payment from the committee to the SMO is for the purpose 
of paying off a debt of over $2,000 that was incurred by the SMO due to printing, voter data, 
postage and design. This payment would be an unearmarked payment to help defray the general 
costs of publishing the slate mailers. Therefore, consideration would not have been exchanged, 
making the payment from the committee a "contribution." Accordingly, the SMO would qualify as 
a general purpose recipient committee under Section 82013 and in tum would need to organize as a 
committee and file campaign statements, in addition to its SMO campaign statements. 
Recipient Committee Reporting 
As noted above, if a slate mailer organization receives $2,000 or more in contributions, it 
will qualify as a general purpose recipient committee under Section 82013(a). The general purpose 
recipient committee would need to file a Statement of Organization (Form 410) with the Secretary 
of State and file a copy with the local filing officer to establish the committee. (Section 84101.) The 
Secretary of State will assign a committee ID number to the new committee. (Section 84101.) After 
the committee is opened, the committee will be required to file campaign reports and statements. 
The SMO would also need to continue filing SMO campaign statements (Form 401). 
When an SMO is also a general purpose recipient committee, it must check the box on its 
SMO Campaign Statement (Form 401) indicating that it is also a general purpose recipient 
committee, attach its most recently filed general purpose recipient committee campaign statement to 
its SMO Form 401 and enter the general purpose recipient committee's ID number on the Form 
401. The general purpose recipient committee's campaign statements must disclose committee 
activity, which in this case would include activity related to the contribution from the· existing 
general purpose committee to pay debt in connection with the general production of the SMO's 
slate mailers. The SMO's campaign statements (Form 401) would continue to disclose SMO 
reportable activity. 
The SMO's recipient committee will be required to file recipient committee campaign 
statements until the recipient committee is no longer engaging in reportable activity and terminates 
the committee. (Section 84214.) With regard to committee termination, Regulation 18404 provides: 
"A treasurer of a committee ... may terminate the committee's status as a committee, only by 
completing the termination section on the Form 410 ... declaring, under penalty of perjury, 
that the committee: 
"(1) Has ceased to received contributioris and make expenditures and does not anticipate 
receiving contributions or making expenditures in the future; 
"(2) Has eliminated or has declared that it has no intention or ability to discharge all of its 
debts, loans received, and other obligations; 
"(3) Has no surplus funds; 
"(4) Has filed all required campaign statements disclosing all reportable transactions." 


KG:aja 
, File No. I-19-233 
Page No. 4 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Si:µcerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
· /{/eral ouns I 
By: 
Kately 
reene 
Counsel, Legal Division
